title: Implement KMS-backed signing and operator keypair hardening
description: Address the critical operator keypair management vulnerabilities. The operator keypair is currently stored as a plaintext JSON file mounted via ExternalSecret, with no rotation, no HSM/KMS backing, and no multi-signature protection. This single key controls all settlement, refund, pause, and unpause operations. This task hardens the keypair lifecycle for the hackathon scope and documents a post-hackathon roadmap for full KMS migration and multisig.
subtasks: Restrict the mounted keypair file permissions to 0400 (read-only by owner) in the Kubernetes pod security context and verify the filesystem is read-only as planned in task 11

Implement a signing abstraction layer in the settlement sidecar (crates/settlement-sidecar/src/signer.rs) with a trait Signer { fn sign(&self, message: &[u8]) -> Signature } supporting both local keypair (hackathon) and KMS-backed (post-hackathon) implementations
Add environment variable SIGNER_TYPE (local|kms) to SidecarConfig to allow switching between local keypair and KMS signer without code changes
Create a stub KMS signer implementation that returns an error with a descriptive message pointing to the upgrade path, so the abstraction is wired end-to-end

Add an operator spending cap check in the settlement sidecar: track cumulative settlement amounts in a rolling window and halt processing with an alert if a configurable threshold is exceeded (e.g., MAX_OPERATOR_SETTLE_PER_HOUR)

Document the single-operator-key risk as a known limitation in a SECURITY.md file at the repo root, including the post-hackathon roadmap: KMS migration (AWS KMS / GCP Cloud KMS with solana-remote-wallet), on-chain multisig requirement for settlements above a threshold, on-chain authority transfer for key rotation, and time-locked settlement windows
Add a Kubernetes CronJob or startup probe that verifies the operator keypair file exists, has correct permissions (0400), and the public key matches the expected on-chain operator authority
acceptance_criteria: The operator keypair file is mounted with file mode 0400 and the pod filesystem is read-only (verified via kubectl exec stat /secrets/operator-keypair.json)
The settlement sidecar compiles and runs with SIGNER_TYPE=local using the existing keypair loading path
The settlement sidecar compiles with SIGNER_TYPE=kms and returns a descriptive error indicating KMS is not yet implemented
The Signer trait is defined and both LocalSigner and StubKmsSigner implement it
An operator spending cap is enforced: if cumulative settlements exceed MAX_OPERATOR_SETTLE_PER_HOUR, the sidecar pauses processing and emits a critical log/alert
SECURITY.md exists at the repo root documenting SEC-001 and SEC-002 with the full post-hackathon remediation roadmap
The keypair verification probe runs successfully on pod startup and fails with a clear error if the file is missing or has wrong permissions

title: Harden ExternalSecret and Kubernetes secret encryption at rest
description: Ensure the operator keypair stored as a Kubernetes secret via ExternalSecret is encrypted at rest in etcd and never logged. Kubernetes secrets are only base64-encoded by default. This task verifies and enforces encryption at rest, adds log scrubbing protections, and tightens the secret lifecycle.
subtasks: Verify that etcd encryption is enabled in the cluster by checking the EncryptionConfiguration resource for aescbc or secretbox providers; document the verification steps and results
If etcd encryption is not enabled, create the EncryptionConfiguration manifest with aescbc provider and document the steps to apply it (or apply if cluster access allows)
Verify the ExternalSecret source (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) uses envelope encryption; document the provider and encryption method

Add a CI pipeline step (shell script or policy check) that greps controller and sidecar log output for base58 patterns matching Solana keypair format (array of 64 numbers or base58 private key strings) and fails the build if found
Evaluate and document whether to migrate from ExternalSecret to CSI Secret Store Driver (secrets-store.csi.k8s.io) for tighter lifecycle control, with a recommendation and implementation plan
Add annotations to the ExternalSecret resource to set a refresh interval and rotation policy if supported by the provider
acceptance_criteria: Documentation confirms etcd encryption is enabled with aescbc or secretbox provider, or a manifest is created to enable it
Documentation confirms the ExternalSecret provider uses envelope encryption
A CI step exists that scans log output for base58 keypair patterns and fails if detected; the step passes on current codebase
A written evaluation of CSI Secret Store Driver vs ExternalSecret exists with a clear recommendation
ExternalSecret resource has refresh interval and rotation annotations configured

title: Add input validation for on-chain string fields and client-side amount safety
description: Add explicit input validation for task_id, package_id, and source_uri in the on-chain program before PDA derivation (not relying solely on Anchor's max_len at serialization). Also harden the web dashboard deposit/withdraw dialogs against integer overflow, dust amounts, and double-submission.
subtasks: Add require!(task_id.len() <= 64, ...) and require!(package_id.len() <= 64, ...) checks in settle_task and register_agent_package instruction handlers BEFORE calling hash_seed(), with a new error variant InputTooLong
Add character validation for task_id and package_id: only allow alphanumeric characters, hyphens, underscores, and periods (regex pattern [a-zA-Z0-9._-]+); add error variant InvalidCharacters
Add validation that source_uri does not contain control characters (bytes < 0x20 except tab) and has a maximum length of 256; add error variant InvalidSourceUri
Add validation that amount > 0 in deposit, withdraw, and settle_task instructions if not already present

In the web dashboard DepositDialog and WithdrawDialog (task 8003): validate that the input amount multiplied by 10^6 does not exceed Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER (9007199254740991) and display an error if it does
Set a minimum deposit/withdrawal amount of 0.01 USDC (10,000 lamports) in both the on-chain program and the web dashboard UI
Disable the submit button immediately on click in DepositDialog and WithdrawDialog, re-enabling only after transaction confirmation or failure to prevent double-submission
Add a confirmation step (secondary dialog or inline confirmation) for deposits or withdrawals exceeding 100 USDC

Write Anchor tests: attempt settle_task with task_id of 100 characters → InputTooLong error; attempt with task_id containing emoji → InvalidCharacters error; attempt deposit with amount=0 → InvalidAmount error
acceptance_criteria: On-chain program rejects task_id and package_id longer than 64 bytes with InputTooLong error before PDA derivation
On-chain program rejects task_id and package_id containing characters outside [a-zA-Z0-9._-] with InvalidCharacters error
On-chain program rejects source_uri containing control characters with InvalidSourceUri error
On-chain program rejects zero-amount deposits, withdrawals, and settlements with InvalidAmount error
Web dashboard prevents submission of amounts that would overflow u64 when converted to lamports
Web dashboard enforces 0.01 USDC minimum for deposits and withdrawals
Web dashboard submit button is disabled during transaction processing and re-enabled on completion/failure
Web dashboard shows a confirmation step for amounts > 100 USDC
All 3+ new Anchor test cases pass

title: Secure Redis stream with authentication, ACLs, and event integrity signing
description: Harden the Redis stream cto:settlements against unauthorized access and event injection. Currently any pod with Redis network access can read settlement data or inject fraudulent settlement events via XADD. This task adds Redis AUTH, Redis ACLs to restrict operations per consumer, and HMAC signing of settlement events for integrity verification.
subtasks: Configure Redis AUTH password via a Kubernetes secret and require it for all Redis connections in both the controller publisher and settlement sidecar consumer
Update the controller's Redis publisher (billing/publisher.rs) to include the AUTH password from config/environment
Update the settlement sidecar's Redis consumer (consumer.rs) to include the AUTH password from config/environment

Configure Redis ACLs: create a user 'cto-publisher' with permissions limited to XADD on cto:settlements and cto:settlements:dlq only; create a user 'cto-consumer' with permissions limited to XREADGROUP and XACK on cto:settlements and XADD on cto:settlements:dlq only
Implement HMAC-SHA256 signing of settlement events in the controller publisher: compute HMAC of the event JSON using a shared secret, include the HMAC as an additional field in the XADD entry
Implement HMAC verification in the settlement sidecar consumer: before processing any event, verify the HMAC matches; reject and route to DLQ events with invalid or missing HMACs
Store the HMAC shared secret as a Kubernetes secret accessible to both the controller and sidecar pods
Ensure the NetworkPolicy from task 9 restricts Redis access to only controller and sidecar pods (verify this is a hard requirement, not optional)

Write integration tests: verify that a Redis client without AUTH is rejected; verify that the consumer user cannot XADD to the main stream; verify that an event with a tampered HMAC is rejected by the sidecar
acceptance_criteria: Redis connections from both controller and sidecar require AUTH and fail without it
Redis ACLs are configured so the publisher user can only XADD and the consumer user can only XREADGROUP/XACK
Settlement events include an HMAC-SHA256 signature computed over the event payload
The settlement sidecar rejects events with invalid or missing HMAC signatures and routes them to the DLQ
NetworkPolicy restricts Redis port access to only controller and sidecar pods
Integration tests verify AUTH enforcement, ACL restrictions, and HMAC validation

title: Add supply chain security: dependency auditing, version pinning, and container image scanning
description: Address supply chain security gaps by adding dependency auditing to CI, pinning Docker base images by digest, pinning Cargo/npm dependency versions, and adding container image vulnerability scanning to the CI pipeline.
subtasks: Add cargo audit as a CI pipeline step that runs on every PR and fails on any CRITICAL or HIGH severity advisory
Add npm audit --audit-level=high as a CI pipeline step for all JavaScript/TypeScript packages (demo/cli, demo/web) that fails on HIGH or above

Pin the settlement sidecar Dockerfile base images by digest: change FROM rust:1.78 to FROM rust:1.78@sha256:<digest> and FROM debian:bookworm-slim to FROM debian:bookworm-slim@sha256:<digest>; document the digest values and how to update them
Review and pin all Cargo.toml dependencies to exact versions or narrow ranges (e.g., change solana-sdk = '1.18' to solana-sdk = '=1.18.x'); ensure Cargo.lock is committed
Evaluate and switch the runtime base image from debian:bookworm-slim to gcr.io/distroless/cc-debian12 to minimize attack surface; verify the sidecar binary runs correctly on distroless
Add a Trivy or Grype container image scan step in the CI pipeline after docker build, configured to fail on CRITICAL and HIGH CVEs
Ensure the Dockerfile sets a non-root USER directive (e.g., USER 1001:1001) in addition to the Kubernetes PodSecurityStandard
Add cargo deny check to CI for license compliance and duplicate dependency detection
Configure Dependabot or Renovate for automated dependency update PRs on the repository
Document the supply chain security tooling in SECURITY.md with instructions for running audits locally
acceptance_criteria: CI pipeline includes cargo audit step that fails on CRITICAL/HIGH advisories and currently passes
CI pipeline includes npm audit step that fails on HIGH+ and currently passes
Settlement sidecar Dockerfile pins both base images by sha256 digest
All Cargo.toml dependencies use exact or narrow version ranges and Cargo.lock is committed
Settlement sidecar builds and runs successfully on distroless base image (or documents why debian-slim is required)
CI pipeline includes Trivy/Grype scan step that fails on CRITICAL/HIGH CVEs and currently passes
Dockerfile includes USER 1001:1001 directive and the binary runs as non-root
cargo deny check passes in CI
Dependabot or Renovate is configured on the repository
SECURITY.md documents all supply chain security tooling

title: Secure S3/MinIO receipt storage with TLS and application-level encryption
description: Ensure receipt JSON uploaded to S3/MinIO is protected both in transit and at rest. Currently, in-cluster communication to MinIO may use HTTP without TLS, and while SSE-S3 encryption at rest is configured, there is no application-level encryption for defense in depth.
subtasks: Verify the MinIO endpoint URL in the controller's SolanaBillingConfig uses HTTPS; if it uses HTTP, configure MinIO with TLS certificates (self-signed for hackathon is acceptable) and update the endpoint
If in-cluster TLS for MinIO is not feasible, verify that the cluster network layer provides encryption (e.g., Cilium with WireGuard or a service mesh with mTLS) and document the finding

Implement application-level envelope encryption for receipt JSON in the controller before S3 upload: generate a per-receipt data encryption key (DEK), encrypt the JSON with AES-256-GCM, optionally wrap the DEK with a KMS key (or store alongside for hackathon); store the encrypted blob
Add a data classification annotation to the S3 bucket or object metadata indicating receipts contain financial PII
Enable S3 bucket access logging for the receipt bucket to create an audit trail of all read/write operations
Add a decryption utility function in the sidecar or a shared library for reading encrypted receipts when needed for verification
Document the encryption scheme (SSE-S3 at rest + TLS in transit + application-level envelope encryption) in SECURITY.md
acceptance_criteria: MinIO endpoint uses HTTPS or cluster network encryption is verified and documented
Receipt JSON is encrypted at the application level before upload to S3 (verified by downloading a raw object and confirming it is not plaintext JSON)
S3 objects have metadata indicating financial PII data classification
S3 bucket access logging is enabled and producing log entries
A decryption utility can successfully decrypt and parse a stored receipt
SECURITY.md documents the full encryption scheme for receipts

title: Implement persistent audit trail for on-chain operator actions
description: Anchor events are ephemeral in Solana transaction logs and can be pruned. If an operator is compromised, reconstructing the timeline of malicious actions depends on volatile transaction history. This task adds persistent off-chain event indexing and on-chain audit PDAs for critical administrative actions.
subtasks: Create an AuditLog PDA account structure in the on-chain program (seed: [b'audit_log', sequence_number]) that records critical admin actions: action_type (Pause/Unpause/AuthorityChange), actor (Pubkey), timestamp, and a data field for action-specific parameters
Modify the pause and unpause instructions to create an AuditLog PDA entry in addition to emitting the Anchor event
If an authority transfer instruction exists or is planned, add AuditLog PDA creation there as well

Implement an off-chain event indexing script (TypeScript or Rust) that subscribes to program logs via WebSocket (connection.onLogs) and persists all Anchor events (TaskSettled, TaskRefunded, Deposited, Withdrawn, pause/unpause) to a durable store (PostgreSQL, or a JSON file for hackathon scope)
Configure Datadog alerts (task 13 integration) for: pause/unpause events, unusual settlement patterns (e.g., more than N settlements per minute), and authority change events
Add a CLI command or script to reconstruct the full operator action timeline from both on-chain AuditLog PDAs and the off-chain event index
Write Anchor tests: pause the program, verify AuditLog PDA is created with correct action_type, actor, and timestamp
acceptance_criteria: AuditLog PDA is created on-chain for every pause and unpause action with correct action_type, actor, and timestamp
An off-chain event indexer script runs and persists Anchor events to a durable store
Datadog alert rules are configured for pause/unpause events and anomalous settlement rates
A timeline reconstruction script can produce a chronological list of all operator actions from both on-chain and off-chain sources
Anchor tests verify AuditLog PDA creation on pause/unpause

title: Add web dashboard access control and data filtering
description: The web dashboard currently exposes all on-chain settlement data to any connected wallet with no access control, providing an aggregated view of business intelligence. For post-hackathon production, add wallet-based authentication and data filtering. For the hackathon, add minimal guardrails.
subtasks: Add a configurable allowlist of authorized wallet addresses (operator/admin wallets) stored as an environment variable NEXT_PUBLIC_AUTHORIZED_WALLETS; if the list is non-empty, restrict dashboard data display to only those wallets
Implement a server-side Next.js API route (/api/settlements) that accepts a wallet signature challenge to authenticate the caller before returning aggregated settlement data
Add a data filtering mode: when a non-operator wallet connects, only show that wallet's own CustomerBalance, their TaskReceipts (filtered by customer field), and public AgentPackage data
Add a wallet signature verification utility that validates a signed message from the connected wallet to prove ownership
Document this as a hackathon limitation in SECURITY.md with the production roadmap: full RBAC with wallet-based roles, server-side API proxy for all data queries, and rate limiting per wallet
acceptance_criteria: When AUTHORIZED_WALLETS is configured, only listed wallets see the full dashboard; other wallets see only their own data
A server-side API route validates wallet signatures before returning data
Non-operator wallets only see their own CustomerBalance and TaskReceipts
SECURITY.md documents the access control limitation and production roadmap

title: Harden daily spending cap against validator timestamp manipulation
description: The daily spending cap rollover uses clock.unix_timestamp / 86400, which is subject to minor manipulation by validators. Add a minimum gap enforcement between daily resets to prevent premature cap resets.
subtasks: Add a minimum gap check in the settle_task daily cap rollover logic: before resetting daily_spent, require that clock.unix_timestamp - (customer_balance.daily_reset_day * 86400) >= 82800 (23 hours), preventing a reset from occurring less than 23 hours after the last reset
Add a new error variant DailyResetTooSoon for the case where the gap check fails
Document the trust assumption in a code comment: validator timestamps are approximately correct; this mitigation prevents premature resets but does not prevent delayed resets

Write an Anchor test that sets daily_reset_day to a value such that less than 23 hours have passed, attempts a settlement that would trigger a reset, and verifies DailyResetTooSoon error (may require clock manipulation or direct account state setup)
Document in SECURITY.md: the validator timestamp manipulation risk, the 23-hour minimum gap mitigation, and the post-hackathon consideration of slot-based time windows
acceptance_criteria: The on-chain program enforces a minimum 23-hour gap between daily spending cap resets
A new error variant DailyResetTooSoon is defined and returned when the gap is insufficient
Code comments document the trust assumption about validator timestamps
SECURITY.md documents the risk and mitigation
An Anchor test verifies the DailyResetTooSoon error case (or documents why it cannot be tested in the current framework)

title: Add rate limiting and RPC proxying for web dashboard
description: The web dashboard makes direct Solana RPC calls from the browser with no rate limiting or caching, which could be abused to amplify RPC costs or cause DoS. Add client-side caching, rate limiting, and optionally server-side RPC proxying.
subtasks: Integrate React Query (TanStack Query) or SWR for all Solana RPC data fetching hooks (useCustomerBalance, useOperatorConfig, settlement feed fetching) with stale-while-revalidate caching and configurable refetch intervals (e.g., 10 seconds for balance, 15 seconds for settlement feed)
Add a client-side rate limiter utility that prevents more than N RPC calls per minute from the browser (e.g., 30 calls/minute) with a queue or debounce mechanism

Create a Next.js API route (/api/rpc-proxy) that proxies Solana RPC calls through the server, enabling server-side rate limiting per IP address using an in-memory rate limiter (e.g., sliding window counter)
Configure the RPC proxy to use a dedicated RPC endpoint (environment variable SOLANA_RPC_URL) rather than the public devnet endpoint, and enforce per-origin rate limits
Add cache headers to the RPC proxy responses for read-only queries (getProgramAccounts, getAccountInfo) to enable CDN or browser caching
Document the rate limiting configuration in the web dashboard README
acceptance_criteria: All data-fetching hooks use React Query or SWR with stale-while-revalidate caching
Client-side rate limiter prevents more than 30 RPC calls per minute from the browser
Next.js API route proxies RPC calls with per-IP rate limiting (e.g., 60 requests/minute/IP)
RPC proxy uses a configurable dedicated RPC endpoint
Read-only RPC responses include appropriate cache headers
Dashboard README documents the rate limiting configuration
